summary: Unify API fetch layer into typed apiRequest wrapper
problem_statement: API calls are scattered across the client codebase with inconsistent error handling, response parsing, and type assertions. Each call site independently constructs URLs, sets headers, parses JSON, and handles errors. A single typed wrapper centralises this logic, reduces duplication, and ensures every API call returns properly typed data with consistent error handling.
in_scope: Create a typed apiRequest<T> wrapper function that handles URL construction, headers, JSON parsing, and error handling
Define the wrapper's error handling contract (throw on non-OK, return typed data on success)
Refactor all API fetch calls in refactored code paths to use the apiRequest wrapper
Ensure the wrapper supports GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, and DELETE methods as needed by existing calls
out_of_scope: Adding new API endpoints or changing existing endpoint contracts
Authentication/authorization header logic beyond what currently exists
Retry logic, request caching, or request deduplication — not in R2 scope
Server-side fetch calls — this is client-side only
requirements: A typed apiRequest<T> function exists that accepts method, URL, optional body, and returns Promise<T>
The wrapper throws a consistent error type on non-OK responses
All API calls in refactored paths route through the apiRequest wrapper
No runtime behaviour changes occur — the wrapper replaces inline fetch logic one-for-one
acceptance_criteria: All API calls in refactored paths route through a single typed apiRequest wrapper
The wrapper handles all HTTP methods used in the application
npm run check passes with no new type errors introduced
Existing API behaviour is preserved exactly
dependencies: P2-R2-07 — API response type interfaces should be defined before the wrapper references them
risks: Some existing fetch calls may have unique patterns (FormData, non-JSON responses) requiring wrapper overloads or escape hatches
implementation_notes: Place the wrapper in a shared lib/api or utils/api module. Use generics so callers specify the expected response type: apiRequest<Task[]>('/api/tasks'). Consider exporting convenience aliases (apiGet, apiPost) if it improves readability.
